text: The surgeon enters the lesser sac and exposes the pancreas. Using a wolf needle, the pancreatic duct is identified, followed by the use of an ultrasonic scalpel to perform a ductotomy. The ductotomy is extended distally, allowing for the retrieval of a large proximal obstructing stone and several smaller stones. The ductotomy is further extended to a length of fifteen centimeters, with additional stones being removed.
The surgeon irrigates the pancreatic duct thoroughly, removing any remaining debris from the ductal lumen.
The surgeon positions the Roux limb adjacent to the ductotomy site and places a back row of 2-0 silk sutures to secure the connection.

The surgeon creates a fifteen-centimeter enterotomy using an ultrasonic scalpel. Two O PDS sutures are applied for the inner layer anastomosis, transitioning to a canal stitch at the corners and extending to the anterior surface to invert the edges of the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins a second PDS suture on the posterior half of the anastomosis, transitioning it to a canal stitch at the corners and carrying it over to the anterior surface. The anterior portion of the anastomosis is completed, ensuring the edges are inverted.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture to complete the second layer of the anterior anastomosis. A stapled jejuno-jejunostomy is performed, followed by the placement of a drain in the lesser sac.
